categoryDescription: Looks at how the ETF is positioned for the future across five plain-English lenses: short-term hold (1-3 years), long-term hold (5-10 years), upside vs price risk vs other categories, sharp-fall protection and recovery, and where the exposure sits in its cycle (with any un-priced catalyst). Each factor is forward-looking but NOT predictive — the prompt expects light web lookup of macro, valuation, yield, and flow data when needed; provided data blocks alone are not enough. All five factors apply to every ETF group. `factorDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and Pass/Fail rule. `groupInstructions` carries the group-specific perspective.

factors.factorDescription: Asks if this fund is a good fit to hold for the next 1-3 years inside its category. Combines two reads: where current valuation or yield sits vs the fund's own multi-year range, AND how fundamentals or yield are trending in the next year or two. The four-quadrant frame: cheap + improving = best setup; cheap + worsening = value-trap risk; expensive + improving = momentum, defendable; expensive + worsening = worst. Pass when valuation or yield is reasonable AND fundamentals or income are flat-to-improving over the window. Fail when valuation is stretched AND fundamentals or yield are clearly worsening.
factors.factorMetrics: indexName, overviewCategory, peRatio, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, duration, topHoldings, sectorBreakdown, strategyText

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Look at forward P/E vs the index's own multi-year range, plus the earnings-revisions trend over the past 1-3 quarters. Cheap with rising revisions is the best 1-3Y setup; expensive with falling revisions is the worst.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector P/E vs the sector's own range, plus sector-specific earnings or growth trend. For thematic funds, also check whether the theme's adoption story is still building or has already peaked.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: These products are not built for a 1-3 year hold. State that plainly. Use this factor only to flag whether the next few weeks-to-months lean with or against the leverage direction.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Look at supply / demand for commodities, or adoption / regulatory progress for crypto, over the next 1-3 years. Spot vs cost-of-production gives a reasonable price floor for industrial commodities.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Look at the underlying index's valuation plus the current volatility regime. Low VIX compresses option premium; a flat-to-mildly-rising underlying with moderate vol is the sweet spot for option-income.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Combine the equity sleeve's valuation with the bond sleeve's SEC yield vs its own range. A balanced mix at reasonable equity valuations and decent bond carry is a healthy 1-3Y setup.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Look at SEC yield vs the fund's own 5-10Y range, plus real yield (SEC yield minus expected inflation). Decent real yield with stable credit quality is a good 1-3Y carry.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Look at credit spreads vs the 10Y median plus the current default-rate trend. Wide spreads with an improving cycle = Pass. Tight spreads with rising defaults = Fail.

factors.factorDescription: Asks if this fund is a good fit to hold for 5-10 years inside its category. Looks at the secular story for the underlying asset class — long-term growth, structural demand, rate cycle, adoption arc — and whether the fund's position in that story is constructive. This is not a market-timing call; it's a 'does the multi-year story still work' call. Pass when the long-arc story for this exposure is solid. Fail when the long-arc story is fading or has structural headwinds with no offsetting positives.

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Read the long-arc growth story for the index's home market — productivity, demographics, structural earnings power. US large-cap, foreign developed, and EM each have different multi-year stories worth naming.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Theme durability is the test — does this sector or theme have 5-10 year structural tailwinds (AI, energy transition, semis, healthcare innovation), or is the story already mature and pricing it in?
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Not a long-term holding. Mark Fail by default and say plainly that the daily-reset mechanic destroys long-term compounding for retail.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: The long-arc story varies by asset: gold has central-bank demand, oil has the energy-transition tug, BTC has the adoption arc. Name the multi-year story that drives this specific asset.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Long horizon needs a sustainable option-premium engine AND a stable underlying. If the 10Y price-only return is flat or down (steady NAV erosion), the fund is not a long-term hold even when headline yield looks attractive.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Long-arc expected return for a typical 60/40 mix at current valuations runs roughly mid-single-digit real. For target-date funds, check whether the published glide path matches the investor's actual retirement horizon.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Long-arc story is the rate cycle + fiscal trajectory + Treasury issuance pressure. Long duration is essentially a multi-year directional rate bet.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Long-arc story is the default-rate trend and credit-cycle normalization. HY defaults tend to rise as rates stay higher for longer — factor that in for multi-year holds.

factors.factorTitle: Upside vs Price Risk (Category vs Other Categories)
factors.factorDescription: Asks how this category is expected to perform vs other categories an investor could pick instead, and whether the expected upside justifies the price risk. The honest comparison is not the fund vs itself — it's the fund's category vs the next-best category for the investor's dollar. Pass when expected upside over the next 1-5 years is in line with or above peer categories for similar price risk. Fail when expected upside is below peer categories AND the price risk is the same or higher.
factors.factorMetrics: overviewCategory, assetClass, indexName, beta, duration, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, return5y, returnsAnnual, strategyText

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare expected 1-5Y total return for broad equity vs bonds, allocation, and commodities. Equity carries higher price risk — Pass when the expected return premium clearly covers it.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare expected sector or theme return to the broad market AND to other sectors. A sector bet takes on concentration risk on top of equity risk — the expected upside has to clear that higher bar.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Upside is the leverage multiple times the underlying move minus daily-reset decay. Compare to simply taking the unleveraged underlying — for most retail holding windows, the unleveraged option wins.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare expected commodity or crypto path to equity and bond categories. These assets swing harder, so the expected upside has to clear the wider risk band.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Upside is structurally capped by the option-writing strategy. Compare expected total return to a plain high-dividend ETF — does the cushioned but capped profile still deliver more total return after fees?
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare expected return to a similar-risk DIY mix and to other allocation peers. The fund earns its keep if the mix or glide path beats a plain blend at lower fee.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare expected total return (yield + price drift) to other duration buckets and to cash / HYSA. Longer duration brings extra return but also extra rate risk — match the trade-off to the investor's horizon.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare expected credit return (yield + carry) to investment-grade bonds and to equity. Credit takes on default risk — Pass only when spread compensation matches or beats the alternative.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at how the fund handles a sharp market fall AND how well it bounces back. Ordinary drawdowns are not the test — only sharp falls. Mark Fail only when the fund falls sharply AND its recovery clearly lags peers or its benchmark. A sharp fall that recovers in line with the index or peers is acceptable for the mandate. Pass when the fund either avoids sharp falls or recovers in line with peers / benchmark. Fail when it falls sharply AND its recovery materially lags.
factors.factorMetrics: drawdown, drawdownDates, captureRatios, returnsAnnual, return1y, return3y, return5y, beta, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Broad equity falls in market shocks — that's expected. Fail only if the fund falls sharply AND recovers slower than the benchmark or peer set (e.g. lagging on a 2020 or 2022 recovery).
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sectors and themes swing harder than the broad market. Fail only when a sharp drop is followed by a clearly weak recovery vs the sector benchmark or peer set.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Sharp falls are amplified by the leverage factor. Recovery is also amplified — but daily-reset decay can keep the fund below the underlying's recovery path. Quote both numbers side-by-side.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Crypto and some commodities can drop 50-80% and take years to recover. Fail when the fund falls sharply AND lags the underlying spot on the way back.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Covered-call funds should fall less than the underlying (cushion) and recover slower (capped upside). Fail only when the cushion didn't show up in the drop AND the fund still lagged on recovery.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: A balanced fund should fall less than 100% equity in a shock. Fail when the worst-year drop is close to pure equity AND recovery is slower than peer 60/40 mixes.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Long-duration IG / Treasury / muni can lose 20%+ in a rate shock. Pass when the drop matches duration math and the fund recovers in line with a duration-matched index.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit sells off sharply in stress windows. Pass when the drop is in line with the matching credit index AND the recovery is in line. Fail when either the drop or the lag is materially worse than peers.

factors.factorDescription: Reads where the fund's exposure sits in its cycle — accumulation, markup, distribution, or markdown — AND whether there is a clear upside catalyst the market hasn't already priced in. A good cycle phase is a Pass on its own; a credible un-priced catalyst is also a Pass even mid-cycle. Hype-peak red flags for thematic / single-country / digital-asset wrappers: sudden AUM surge + narrative saturation + stretched valuations + breadth narrowing to a few names = late distribution. Pass when the exposure is in accumulation / early markup, OR has a credible upside catalyst not yet in the price. Fail when the exposure is in late distribution / markdown AND no fresh catalyst is visible.
factors.factorMetrics: overviewCategory, indexName, sectorBreakdown, topHoldings, ma50, ma200, rsiM, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent, aum, strategyText

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Read the broad index — price vs MA200, breadth, sentiment, valuation vs its own history. Accumulation / early markup with broad participation = Pass. Late distribution (narrow breadth + crowded long + top-decile valuation) = caution.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Read the specific sector or theme's own cycle. Look for hype-peak signals (peak AUM + peak P/E + narrative saturation) and for early-cycle signs (low AUM + improving fundamentals + reasonable valuation). Name any un-priced upside catalyst (regulatory shift, technology inflection, adoption tipping point).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Cycle the underlying, not the leveraged product itself. Long-leveraged funds win in markup phases; inverse funds win in markdown. Choppy distribution / accumulation phases hurt both.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Each underlying has its own cycle — gold's real-rate cycle, oil's OPEC cycle, BTC's halving cycle. Name the cycle position and any un-priced catalyst (central-bank buying shift, supply disruption, regulatory clarity).
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Cycle the underlying index plus the volatility regime. Low-vol grinding markets compress option income; choppy markets with moderate vol are the sweet spot for option-writing strategies.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: The cycle is the blend of equity and bond cycles. A balanced fund near a rate peak with reasonable equity valuations has both sleeves working — that's a healthy cycle position.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Cycle the rate path — yields near multi-year highs with the Fed near pause is the strongest setup for duration. Falling-rate cycles favor long duration; rising-rate cycles favor short duration or floating-rate.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Cycle the credit market — wide spreads with an improving economy = early cycle Pass. Tight spreads with deteriorating credit = late cycle / distribution. Watch for un-priced catalysts (Fed cuts, default-rate turn).
